__includedFiles.README.md:
# 203669-rds-cloud-iac  - RDS-Tool

This project Provisions RDS Instances to Shared (a206645) Namespace.

## Prerequisites

- [Python](https://realpython.com/installing-python/)
  - Version 3.7 is currently the recommended version for use by cloud-iac.
- cloud-iac
  - [Install the latest version of cloud-iac](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_cloud-iac/blob/v2/docs/installation/installation.md) on your local workstation.
  - Review the [cloud-iac Quick Start](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_cloud-iac/blob/v2/docs/quickstart/quickstart.md) guide if you are new to cloud-iac.
- [git](https://github.com/git-guides/install-git)
  - Install the most recent version of git for your platform.
- Access to AWS
  - We recommend running the CodePipeline from a CI/CD account and targeting a BU account for creating Aurora clusters.
- CodeStar GitHub connection
  - A CodeStar GitHub connection must exist in the AWS account from which the CodePipeline will run.
  - Reference [this document](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_hub-docs/blob/main/aws-cicd/codepipeline-github.md) to determine which CodeStar GitHub connection to use, or, if one does not exist, request a new CodeStar GitHub connection by following the steps in the document.

## Getting Started

The following are the steps required to set up a new copy of this project.

1. Create new GitHub repository.
2. Clone [RDS-Tool](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_rds-tool) repository to your desired local folder.
3. Open and modify the following config files for your use case.
    - variables.yaml
    - config/config.yaml
    - config/prod/config.yaml

Note: Change the Directory structure according to your project/environment naming standards..

4. Platform Engineering recommends deploying the pipeline from a CI-CD AWS account. When deploying from CI-CD, set the *iam_role* variable in the *config/dev/config.yaml* configuration file with the ARN of the PowerUser2 role in the target BU account.
  - Example of configuring the pipeline to use a PowerUser2 role in a BU AWS account:
   ```yaml
   iam_role : arn:aws:iam:<BU aws account id>:role/human-role/<a{AssetInsightId}>-PowerUser2
   ```
5. After modifications are complete login to the correct account using `cloud-tool login`, then deploy the pipeline using `cloud-iac deploy-pipeline`.
6. *(Not recommended)* If you prefer to deploy from a BU AWS account instead, then you will need to manually create an IAM service role as follows:
   - Use the CloudFormation template that was generated in the following location *cf-pipeline/deployment-iam-role.json* to create a new stack for the new IAM role.
   - Set the value of the *iam_role* variable in the *config/dev/config.yaml* configuration file with the ARN of the role that you just created. 
   - Example of configuring the pipeline to use the manually created IAM service role in a BU AWS account:
   ```yaml
   iam_role : arn:aws:iam:<BU aws account id>:role/service-role/<a{AssetInsightId}-{ProjectCode}>-deploy
   ```
   - After modifications are complete, redeploy the pipeline using `cloud-iac deploy-pipeline`.
7. Once deployed, push to the newly created repository using the following commands:
```
git add .
git commit -m "your commit msg here"
git push -u origin main
```
8. The pipeline will execute then trigger a CodeBuild project.

## Postgres Extensions 

These instructions detail how to install Postgres extensions for your Aurora database cluster. 

Install the latest version of rds_tool to your local workstation using pip. 

```shell
pip install rds-tool --upgrade
```

After installing the rds_tool package in your local machine use cloud-tool to log in into the AWS account in which your Aurora databse cluster exists. After successfully loggin in to the AWS account, use cloud-tool's
tunneling functionality to establish a connection to the cluster.

Note: The cluster to which you're connecting must have the Postgresql Bastion security group attached to it.


```shell
## cloud tool tunneling
cloud-tool ssh-tunnel -I -c <cluster writer endpoint>  -r 5432
```

After successfully tunnelling to your cluster, use rds-tool's ```pg-extensions``` CLI command to create extensions against the target cluster.

We recommend installing the following extensions:
- pgaudit
- uuid-ossp
- pg_partman

The following are the required parameters for the ``pg-extensions`` command:
```shell
pg-extensions <ext 1>,<ext 2> ... <ext n> -c <cluster-name> -s <secret-name> -r <aws-region>
````
Alternatively, you can set the cluster name and secret name via environment variables: 

```shell
export CLUSTER_NAME=<cluster-name>
export SECRET_NAME=<secret-name>

pg-extensions <ext 1>,<ext 2> ... <ext n>
```

Example:
```shell
pg-extensions pgaudit,uuid-ossp,pg_partman -c a204503-rds-tool-cloud-iac-demo-ok-to-delete -s a204503-rds-tool-cloud-iac-demo-ok-to-delete -r us-east-1
```


## RDS Tool Configs
This pipeline is designed to ingest YAML configuration files. These YAML files will inform RDS-Tool as to which AWS resources to provision. These configuration files need to be stored inside the rds_tool_config/{environment}/{config.yaml} folder. The environments must match the env parameter set in the database.yaml file. Example config files can be found in the [rds_tool_configs/examples](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_rds-tool-cloud-iac/tree/main/rds_tool_configs/examples) folder.


## FAQ

## More Resources
See the [wiki](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_rds-tool-cloud-iac/wiki)  for more details.

This project utilizes the [RDS-Tool library](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_rds-tool/). More details about RDS-Tool and what it supports are available in the [RDS-Tool Wiki](https://github.com/tr/nuvola_rds-tool/wiki).

## Unicode Control Characters

         

The following characters can sometimes cause issues with database storage or string processing:

- **Substitute (\u001A)**: Historically used as an End-Of-File (EOF) marker.
- **Backspace (\u0008)**: Can cause issues in specific display or logging contexts.
- **Surrogate Pairs (Unpaired) (\uD800 through \uDFFF)**: Invalid or lone surrogate halves are technically invalid UTF-8.
- **Byte Order Mark (\uFEFF)**: Can be misinterpreted as a header rather than content.
